era_context: The Thirty Years' War was in its seventeenth year, with France now openly entering the conflict against the Habsburgs. The Ming Dynasty in China was in its final decades, facing internal decay and external Manchu pressure. In Europe, the Scientific Revolution continued to develop, with Galileo under house arrest and new ideas circulating. Colonial expansion in North America was accelerating, with English settlements expanding and conflicts with indigenous peoples increasing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior of Southeast Asia and Oceania (sparse records)

events.title: France declares war on Spain and the Holy Roman Empire
events.description: France, under Cardinal Richelieu, formally declared war on Spain and the Holy Roman Empire, openly entering the Thirty Years' War on the side of the Protestant powers. This declaration transformed the conflict from a primarily German religious war into a broader European struggle for hegemony. French entry brought significant military resources and prolonged the war, ultimately contributing to the exhaustion of the Habsburgs and the reshaping of European power balances.

sources.name: French royal declarations and diplomatic correspondence
Spanish and Imperial responses and records

events.certainty_note: The declaration date and circumstances are well-documented in diplomatic archives and contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Battle of the Lech River
events.description: Swedish forces under Field Marshal Johan Banér, supported by French allies, defeated the Imperial army at the Lech River in Bavaria. The victory demonstrated the effectiveness of the Swedish-French alliance and further weakened Imperial military capacity. The battle resulted in significant Swedish territorial gains and contributed to the broader pattern of Habsburg military decline during the Thirty Years' War.

sources.name: Swedish military records and contemporary accounts

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-documented in military records from multiple sources; date and outcome are uncontested.

events.title: Tokugawa Iemitsu establishes sakoku (closed country) policy
events.description: The Tokugawa shogun Iemitsu formalized and intensified the sakoku (closed country) policy, severely restricting foreign trade and contact with the outside world. This policy, which had been developing since the early 1630s, was now systematically enforced through edicts limiting foreign merchants and prohibiting Japanese from traveling abroad. The sakoku policy would remain in effect for over two centuries, profoundly shaping Japanese isolation and internal development during the Edo period.
key_figures: Tokugawa Ieyasu (founder of the shogunate, deceased but his policies continued)

sources.name: Tokugawa shogunate edicts and official records (Bakufu documents)
Japanese chronicles and later historical compilations

events.certainty_note: The sakoku policy developed gradually; 1635 represents a key year of formalization, though some sources cite 1633 or 1638 as pivotal dates.

events.title: Connecticut Colony established
events.description: English settlers, primarily from Massachusetts Bay Colony, established the Connecticut Colony through the founding of settlements at Hartford, Windsor, and Wethersfield. The colony was formally organized under the Fundamental Orders (adopted in 1638–1639), which established a representative government. Connecticut's establishment represented the expansion of English colonial presence in New England and the beginning of systematic English settlement in the Connecticut River valley.

sources.name: Connecticut colonial records and town records
Massachusetts Bay Colony records documenting emigration

events.certainty_note: The founding of Connecticut settlements occurred in 1635–1636; the exact date of formal establishment is sometimes given as 1636 or 1638.

events.title: Galileo Galilei remains under house arrest in Arcetri
events.description: Galileo continued his confinement under house arrest in Arcetri, near Florence, following his 1633 trial by the Inquisition. Despite his restrictions, Galileo continued his scientific work and correspondence, including work on mechanics and the motion of bodies. His continued intellectual activity during this period, despite censorship and isolation, demonstrated the resilience of scientific inquiry and contributed to the eventual acceptance of heliocentrism and modern physics.
key_figures: Pope Urban VIII (who had ordered the confinement)

sources.name: Galileo's correspondence and manuscripts from this period

events.certainty_note: Galileo's house arrest is well-documented; his continued work during this period is attested in his letters and manuscripts.

events.title: Ming Dynasty faces severe fiscal crisis
events.description: The Ming Dynasty experienced acute fiscal and administrative crises, with widespread tax evasion, corruption, and military expenditures draining the imperial treasury. The government struggled to fund military campaigns against the Manchus in the north and suppress internal rebellions. This fiscal collapse contributed to the weakening of Ming military capacity and administrative coherence, accelerating the dynasty's decline toward its fall in 1644.

sources.name: Ming imperial records and court documents (Ming Shilu)

events.certainty_note: The fiscal crisis of the 1630s is well-documented in Ming records, though the specific year 1635 is part of a broader crisis period (1630s–1644).

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of France's 1635 declaration of war is sometimes overstated in narratives that emphasize French agency; France had been covertly supporting anti-Habsburg forces for years before the formal declaration. The sakoku policy's implementation date is disputed; some scholars argue that the policy was not fully formalized until 1638 or later, and that 1635 represents only one stage in a gradual process. Connecticut's founding date is ambiguous; some sources date the colony's establishment to 1636 or even 1638, depending on whether one counts the initial settlements or the formal adoption of the Fundamental Orders. The characterization of Galileo's house arrest as merely 'confinement' obscures the fact that he maintained significant intellectual activity and correspondence, suggesting his isolation was less complete than sometimes portrayed.
historiographic_note: The year 1635 is well-documented in European sources, particularly regarding the Thirty Years' War and early colonial North America. Chinese sources on the Ming fiscal crisis are abundant but are less frequently consulted in Western historiography. Japanese sources on the sakoku policy are detailed but often interpreted through the lens of later Western narratives about Japanese 'isolation.' The historical record is heavily weighted toward European and European-colonial perspectives, with limited documentation of internal developments in Asia and Africa. Indigenous American perspectives on colonial expansion are largely absent from contemporary written records.

graph_edges.note: French entry into the Thirty Years' War prolonged the conflict and shaped the eventual Peace of Westphalia
Swedish-French victories at the Lech River contributed to the broader pattern of Swedish dominance in northern Germany
Sakoku policy established a pattern of Japanese isolation that lasted until the Perry Expedition
Connecticut's establishment contributed to the development of colonial New England and later American independence
Galileo's continued work during house arrest influenced the eventual acceptance of heliocentrism
Ming fiscal crisis accelerated the dynasty's collapse and enabled Manchu conquest
